text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP VISIT
Nivolumab Rechallenge / First Maintenance Dose

ATTENDING: Medical Oncology
PATIENT: Renata Vasquez-Okonkwo
AGE: 56 years
DIAGNOSIS: Stage IV (M1c) cutaneous malignant melanoma, superficial spreading type, BRAF V600E-mutant, PD-L1 45% TPS, TMB-high (42 mut/Mb)

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo presents to the medical oncology clinic today for her first nivolumab (Opdivo) maintenance infusion following successful completion of her oral prednisone steroid taper. She was cleared for rechallenge after confirmatory flexible sigmoidoscopy demonstrated mucosal healing and resolution of immune-mediated colitis. Today also serves as a comprehensive reassessment of her overall clinical status in the wake of her recent hospitalization and toxicity management course.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo is a 56-year-old woman with a history of hypothyroidism (previously on levothyroxine 75 mcg) and mixed Afro-Caribbean/Eastern European descent who was diagnosed with Stage IV BRAF V600E-mutant cutaneous melanoma originating from the left posterior shoulder (Breslow thickness 4.2 mm, ulcerated, Clark level V, 4 mitoses/mm2, lymphovascular invasion present) with metastases to bilateral axillary lymph nodes, multiple subcutaneous nodules, and four hepatic lesions at initial presentation. Her LDH at diagnosis was elevated at 387 U/L.

She was initially treated with first-line dabrafenib (Tafinlar) 150 mg BID plus trametinib (Mekinist) 2 mg daily, achieving a significant partial response (roughly 68% reduction in measurable disease) by six months with normalization of LDH. This was complicated by two episodes of drug-related pyrexia — the second of which (temperature 39.1°C with rigors) necessitated a dabrafenib dose reduction to 100 mg BID — as well as grade 1 hand-foot syndrome and grade 2 peripheral edema. Despite initial excellent response, repeat CT imaging at approximately eight months demonstrated clear progression: enlarging axillary lymphadenopathy, growth and a new hepatic lesion, and two new small pulmonary nodules in the right lower lobe. Her LDH had risen to 512 U/L.

Given acquired resistance to BRAF/MEK inhibition and her favorable tumor immunologic profile (PD-L1 45%, TMB 42 mut/Mb), she was transitioned to second-line combination immunotherapy with ipilimumab 1 mg/kg plus nivolumab 3 mg/kg IV every three weeks (CheckMate-067 dosing schema). She completed two cycles without significant adverse effects before developing grade 3 immune-mediated colitis during cycle 3 (approximately 6 weeks after initiation) — presenting with 10-12 watery/bloody bowel movements daily, severe cramping, fever, and elevated CRP of 94 mg/L. She was admitted to the hospital, infectious etiologies were thoroughly excluded, and flexible sigmoidoscopy confirmed diffuse moderate-to-severe colitis from the rectum to the descending colon. She was treated with IV methylprednisolone 2 mg/kg/day with good response, transitioned to oral prednisone 1 mg/kg/day (80 mg daily), and discharged on hospital day 5. Ipilimumab was permanently discontinued. Nivolumab was held pending completion of the steroid taper.

Despite the treatment interruption, CT imaging obtained approximately 10 weeks from cycle 1 of combination immunotherapy showed a meaningful partial response — right axillary lymph node decreased from 1.6 cm to 0.9 cm, left axilla from 1.1 cm to 0.6 cm, hepatic lesions shrank (right lobe: 2.3 to 1.4 cm, 1.4 to 0.7 cm), the newly appeared hepatic lesion was no longer identifiable, and the right lower lobe pulmonary nodules decreased to 0.3 and 0.2 cm. LDH trended down to 241 U/L.

Over the subsequent weeks, Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo completed her prednisone taper successfully. Repeat flexible sigmoidoscopy (performed approximately 4 weeks ago) demonstrated complete mucosal healing with no endoscopic evidence of active colitis, confirming clinical remission and clearing her for nivolumab rechallenge. Prednisone was discontinued, with her final dose below 10 mg/day at the time of today's visit.

She now presents for her first dose of nivolumab 480 mg IV flat-dose every 4 weeks in the maintenance setting. She reports feeling considerably better than she did at the peak of her colitis. She has regained approximately 4 pounds since discharge and reports a normalized bowel pattern.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Gastrointestinal: Bowel habits normalized — 1 to 2 formed, well-formed bowel movements per day, no loose stools, no blood or mucus in stool, no urgency or tenesmus. No abdominal pain or cramping. Denies nausea, vomiting, or early satiety. No dysphagia.

Constitutional: Fatigue is mild and improving, ECOG PS 1 by patient self-report (though she is close to ECOG 0 functionally). Appetite returned to near-baseline. Reports weight gain of approximately 4 pounds since discharge.

Endocrine/Thyroid: She endorses mild fatigue, occasional cold intolerance, and mild constipation — symptoms potentially consistent with mild hypothyroidism. She was on levothyroxine 75 mcg at the time of initial diagnosis; her dosage was increased to 100 mcg at her prior visit in anticipation of checkpoint inhibitor-related thyroiditis.

Respiratory: No dyspnea at rest. Denies cough, hemoptysis, or chest pain. Previously noted right lower lobe pulmonary nodules appear to be responding on last imaging.

Musculoskeletal: No joint pain, stiffness, or new myalgias. No peripheral edema (was present grade 2 during targeted therapy, resolved after discontinuation).

Dermatologic: No new skin lesions noted by patient. No rash.

Neurologic: No headache, visual changes, focal weakness, or sensory disturbance.

Genitourinary: No dysuria, hematuria. Normal urine output.

Hematologic: No unusual bruising or bleeding.

All other systems reviewed and negative.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY (previously documented, updated as relevant)
- Stage IV BRAF V600E-mutant cutaneous malignant melanoma, left posterior shoulder (diagnosed age 55) — currently on active treatment
- Hypothyroidism — on levothyroxine, likely compounded now by immune-mediated thyroiditis from checkpoint inhibitor therapy
- Prior immune-mediated colitis, grade 3, from ipilimumab/nivolumab (now resolved, mucosal healing confirmed)
- 10 pack-year smoking history, quit 20 years prior
- No prior cardiac, pulmonary, or hepatic disease

SOCIAL HISTORY
- Works as a landscape architect; currently on modified duty with reduced hours due to recent hospitalization but anticipates full return to work soon
- Significant occupational and recreational sun exposure history
- Non-smoker (former, quit 20 years ago)
- Alcohol: rare, social; denies illicit drug use
- Lives with partner; supportive family structure
- Mixed Afro-Caribbean and Eastern European heritage

FAMILY HISTORY
- Previously documented; no known family history of melanoma, BRCA-related malignancies, or other hereditary cancer syndromes

ALLERGIES
- No known drug allergies
- Ipilimumab: permanently discontinued (not a true allergy — related adverse event, grade 3 colitis)

MEDICATIONS
1. Levothyroxine 100 mcg orally once daily (dose increased at prior visit from 75 mcg; today's TSH will guide further adjustment)
2. Omeprazole 20 mg orally once daily (initiated during prednisone course for GI protection; continuing briefly post-taper)
3. Calcium carbonate 500 mg with vitamin D 400 IU orally twice daily (initiated during prednisone course for bone protection; to be reassessed)
4. Acetaminophen PRN (mild discomfort)
5. Prednisone — completed taper; final dose was 5 mg daily, discontinued approximately 10 days ago
6. Nivolumab 480 mg IV every 4 weeks — FIRST DOSE TODAY

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 118/74 mmHg, heart rate 74 bpm, respiratory rate 16, temperature 37.0°C, oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Weight: 67.2 kg (up approximately 4 lbs from discharge weight). Height: 165 cm. BMI: 24.7.

General: Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo is a well-appearing, pleasant woman in no acute distress. She is alert, oriented, and conversant. Appears to have returned to close to her baseline weight and health.

Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Sclerae non-icteric, conjunctivae non-injected. Oropharynx clear. Mucous membranes moist.

Neck: No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. Thyroid gland without palpable nodules or goiter. No JVD.

Lymphatics: No palpable axillary lymphadenopathy bilaterally on careful examination — a significant improvement from prior visits when bilateral axillary lymph nodes were clearly palpable. No inguinal lymphadenopathy.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Peripheral pulses 2+ throughout.

Respiratory: Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheeze, crackle, or rhonchi. Good air entry.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Bowel sounds active in all four quadrants. No hepatosplenomegaly appreciated on palpation. No voluntary or involuntary guarding. No rebound tenderness. Surgical scar from primary melanoma excision (left posterior shoulder) healing well without signs of local recurrence.

Skin: No new suspicious pigmented lesions identified on visual inspection of exposed skin. Excision scar on left posterior shoulder stable. No rash, no erythema, no desquamation. No new subcutaneous nodules palpated along right flank or axillary regions.

Neurologic: Alert and oriented x4. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Motor strength 5/5 in all extremities. Sensation intact to light touch. Gait normal. No focal deficits.

Extremities: No peripheral edema (previously grade 2 during targeted therapy, now fully resolved). No clubbing or cyanosis.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (obtained today, pre-infusion)

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC: 6.4 x10^9/L (normal)
- Hemoglobin: 12.8 g/dL (mild, stable — likely residual effect from prior colitis/anemia; previously 10.2 g/dL at hospitalization, improving)
- Platelets: 214 x10^9/L (normal)
- Neutrophils: 3.9 x10^9/L; lymphocytes: 1.8 x10^9/L

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium 139, potassium 4.0, chloride 102, bicarbonate 25, BUN 14, creatinine 0.79 mg/dL
- Glucose: 95 mg/dL (normal; checking given recent prolonged steroid use — reassuring)
- ALT: 24 U/L, AST: 21 U/L, alkaline phosphatase: 68 U/L, total bilirubin: 0.7 mg/dL — all within normal limits
- Albumin: 4.0 g/dL (improving from mild hypoalbuminemia during hospitalization)

LDH: 228 U/L (continues to trend toward normal range; upper limit of normal approximately 240 U/L at our lab — essentially normalized)

Thyroid Function:
- TSH: 6.8 mIU/L — mildly elevated (normal 0.4–4.0 mIU/L), consistent with mild hypothyroidism, likely representing immune-related thyroiditis from prior checkpoint inhibitor therapy
- Free T4: to be resulted (sent today)
- This is a new finding compared to prior TSH of 2.1 mIU/L at baseline and supports the diagnosis of checkpoint inhibitor-associated thyroiditis

Cortisol (morning): 14.2 mcg/dL — within normal range, reassuring given prolonged steroid course; no adrenal insufficiency at this time

Urinalysis: Clear, no blood, no protein, no glucose, specific gravity 1.015

---

IMAGING RESULTS

Most recent CT chest/abdomen/pelvis (approximately 2 weeks prior): The imaging taken during the steroid taper period demonstrated a meaningful partial response to combination immunotherapy — axillary lymph nodes decreased to 0.9 cm (right) and 0.6 cm (left) from post-progression highs of 1.6 cm and 1.1 cm respectively; the hepatic lesions in the right lobe shrank noticeably (from 2.3 cm to 1.4 cm, and from 1.4 cm to 0.7 cm); and importantly, the new right hepatic lesion that appeared at progression was no longer identifiable. The right lower lobe pulmonary nodules — which had been a concerning new finding at progression — have decreased to the point of near-resolution (0.3 cm and 0.2 cm). MRI brain showed no intracranial disease.

These findings are encouraging, particularly given that this response was achieved largely on the combined platform with some residual benefit persisting even through the treatment interruption for toxicity management.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo is a 56-year-old woman with Stage IV BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic cutaneous melanoma who has had a clinically meaningful and durable partial response to ipilimumab/nivolumab combination immunotherapy, complicated by grade 3 immune-mediated colitis requiring hospitalization and high-dose steroids. She has now successfully completed her prednisone taper, had mucosal healing confirmed on sigmoidoscopy, and presents today for her first nivolumab (Opdivo) flat-dose 480 mg IV rechallenge as maintenance monotherapy. Overall status appears quite favorable.

1. METASTATIC MELANOMA — ACTIVE TREATMENT:
She has demonstrated a substantial partial response to combination checkpoint inhibitor therapy, with meaningful shrinkage of axillary, hepatic, and pulmonary disease. Given her high PD-L1 (45% TPS), very high TMB (42 mutations/Mb), and the durable nature of the partial response observed even during the treatment break, nivolumab rechallenge with monotherapy is clinically appropriate and supported by published rechallenge data and ASCO/NCCN guidelines. Per standard practice for flat-dosing, she will receive nivolumab 480 mg IV over 30 minutes today. Premedication with diphenhydramine and acetaminophen administered. Vital signs and infusion-related reaction precautions in place. Plan to continue nivolumab 480 mg IV every 4 weeks with restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and MRI brain every 12 weeks. Ipilimumab is permanently discontinued and will not be re-administered given the grade 3 colitis.

2. IMMUNE-RELATED HYPOTHYROIDISM / THYROIDITIS:
Today's TSH is 6.8 mIU/L, newly elevated compared to her pre-immunotherapy baseline TSH of 2.1 mIU/L. Free T4 pending. This is consistent with checkpoint inhibitor-associated thyroiditis, a well-known class effect. Her prior levothyroxine was increased from 75 mcg to 100 mcg at our last visit in anticipation of this. She reports mild symptoms (fatigue, cold intolerance, occasional constipation) potentially attributable to mild hypothyroidism. Given TSH of 6.8 mIU/L in the context of symptoms, I am increasing levothyroxine dose to 100 mcg daily (she was already told to take 100 mcg; confirming dose) — will wait for free T4 result before further titration. If free T4 is low, will consider increasing to 112 or 125 mcg. Endocrinology referral placed today. Thyroid function to be rechecked in 6 weeks. No nivolumab dose interruption indicated for grade 1-2 hypothyroidism managed with hormone replacement. Patient counseled on symptoms of both hypothyroidism and hyperthyroidism (the latter can precede the hypothyroid phase of thyroiditis).

3. PRIOR IMMUNE-RELATED COLITIS — MONITORING:
Colitis is clinically in remission — she reports 1-2 formed bowel movements daily, no blood, no pain. Steroid taper completed successfully. GI follow-up scheduled at 6 weeks for clinical reassessment. Patient re-educated on the importance of immediately reporting any increase in stool frequency, presence of blood in stool, abdominal cramping, or fever — and to report directly to the emergency department with low threshold given the prior severity. Nivolumab rechallenge after grade 3 colitis from combination therapy (with ipilimumab permanently discontinued) is consistent with standard practice; risk of colitis recurrence with nivolumab monotherapy is substantially lower than with combination therapy. Patient understands and accepts the risk.

4. STEROID-RELATED CONSIDERATIONS:
Given approximately 8-10 weeks of systemic corticosteroids (initially high-dose IV, then oral prednisone taper), the following are addressed:
- Blood glucose today normal at 95 mg/dL — reassuring, no new-onset diabetes
- Morning cortisol 14.2 mcg/dL — normal adrenal function, no adrenal insufficiency
- Bone protection: Patient will continue calcium carbonate 500 mg with vitamin D 400 IU twice daily for an additional 3 months. Will obtain a DEXA scan in 3 months given prolonged steroid exposure if she has not had one recently. No bisphosphonate initiated at this time.
- Omeprazole 20 mg daily — will continue for 2 more weeks, then discontinue as steroid course is complete and GI mucosa healed

5. SURVEILLANCE FOR NEW PRIMARY MELANOMA:
Referred to dermatology for semi-annual full-body skin surveillance. Given history of primary cutaneous melanoma and significant cumulative sun exposure history in her occupation, ongoing skin surveillance remains critical. Patient reminded to use broad-spectrum SPF 50+ sunscreen daily, wear protective clothing, and avoid peak-hour sun when possible.

6. PERFORMANCE STATUS AND QUALITY OF LIFE:
Patient is ECOG PS 1 today, improving (was ECOG PS 1 at recent hospitalization). Weight improving. She is not yet back to full-time work as a landscape architect but expects to resume within the coming weeks. Referred to survivorship support services. Nutritional counseling offered to continue weight recovery. Psychological support offered; patient declines at this time, noting strong social support.

7. LABS/FOLLOW-UP:
- Free T4 result to be called to patient when resulted
- LFTs, CBC, CMP, and LDH to be checked prior to each nivolumab infusion
- TSH in 6 weeks
- Morning cortisol (repeat in 2-3 months if symptoms suggest adrenal insufficiency)
- CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast + MRI brain in approximately 12 weeks for restaging
- GI follow-up in 6 weeks
- Endocrinology referral placed for thyroid management
- Dermatology referral placed for skin surveillance

Total visit time: 55 minutes (>50% time spent on counseling and coordination of care).

Patient verbalized understanding of the above plan, agrees to proceed with nivolumab infusion today, and was given printed materials on checkpoint inhibitor toxicity management. She was reminded to call the oncology nurse line immediately for any new symptoms and to go to the emergency department for fever, severe diarrhea, or respiratory symptoms. She leaves in stable condition.

Cancer status: present, partial response maintained on checkpoint inhibitor therapy.
